title: Worst-case (dense) graphs: reach the clique-optimal Θ(εn) scaling for all G

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
**Gap / tightness check.** Numerics show the complete graph $K_n$ has maximum $\varepsilon$-light fraction exactly $\varepsilon$ ($\max|S|=\lfloor\varepsilon n\rfloor$, verified $n\le20$). So: (i) necessarily $c\le1$, and (ii) the linear-in-$\varepsilon$ order is tight, with cliques (and expanders) the stress cases.

A complete proof must confirm the selection mechanism achieves $|S|=\Omega(\varepsilon n)$ for **dense** graphs, not only sparse ones. Specifically: verify the argument on $K_n$ recovers $|S|=\Theta(\varepsilon n)$ (the numerics indicate vanilla density-$\varepsilon$ sampling is borderline here, so the chosen mechanism must demonstrably clear the clique). If the proof splits into sparse vs. dense cases, prove the dense case (where induced edges are abundant) attains the bound with the same universal $c$.

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
## Worst-case (dense) scaling — detailed spec

Numerics: for $K_n$, $\varepsilon L_{K_n}\succeq L_{K_k}$ iff $\varepsilon\ge k/n$, so the largest $\varepsilon$-light set in $K_n$ has size exactly $\lfloor\varepsilon n\rfloor$. Hence (a) $c\le1$, and (b) the construction must achieve $\Theta(\varepsilon n)$ on dense graphs and expanders, not just on sparse graphs (stars/bounded-degree are easy). Calibrate $c$ against this extremal example and verify no degradation when every vertex has large effective degree / leverage.
